title: Speaking & Listening
objective: Students will explore speaking & listening concepts including Preparing for and Explaining in Discussions, Reporting & Recounting.

lessons.title: Preparing for and Explaining in Discussions

steps.content: Welcome to an exciting lesson about Preparing for and Explaining in Discussions!
Come to discussions prepared, draw on preparation and known information, and explain ideas in light of the discussion
Let me show you something cool. Read assigned material and prepare three discussion points or questions before a group conversation
If you knows they're going to discuss a topic in class, do they think about it beforehand and come ready with ideas or facts to share?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Preparing for and Explaining in Discussions: Read assigned material and prepare three discussion points or questions before a group conversation During discussion, refer explicitly to the text or preparation notes to support a point
Let's explore Reporting & Recounting! Have you ever wondered about this?
Report on a topic or recount an experience with organised facts and descriptive details, speaking clearly at an understandable pace
Let's practice together! Deliver a one-minute oral report on a chosen topic, including at least three organised facts
Can you give a short talk about a topic they've researched — presenting the key facts in a logical order and speaking clearly enough for others to follow?

Great job! Today we learned about Reporting & Recounting. Deliver a one-minute oral report on a chosen topic, including at least three organised facts Recount a personal experience using descriptive details and a clear beginning, middle, and end

lessons.title: Spellings from Greek, French and Latin

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of Spellings from Greek, French and Latin!
Spell words with etymological letter patterns from Greek (ch = /k/), French (ch = /ʃ/, -gue, -que), and Latin (sc = /s/) origins
Time to try this out: Spell words with Greek ch such as 'scheme', 'chorus', 'chemist', 'echo' and explain the /k/ sound pattern
Does you know that some words have letter patterns from other languages — like "ch" making a "k" sound in "school" or "chorus" — and can they spell those words correctly?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Spellings from Greek, French and Latin: Spell words with Greek ch such as 'scheme', 'chorus', 'chemist', 'echo' and explain the /k/ sound pattern Spell words with French ch such as 'chef', 'chalet', 'machine' and -gue/-que endings like 'league', 'unique'
